questions.question:
In the following grammar:
  X ::= X @ Y | Y
  Y ::= Z * Y | Z
  Z ::= id

Which of the following is true?
A. @ is left associative while * is right associative
B. Both @ and * are left associative
C. @ is right associative while * is left associative
D. None of the above
questions.answer: A. @ is left associative while * is right associative
questions.to_find: The associativity of operators @ and * as defined by the given grammar.

understand.plain: A grammar production tells us how an operator associates. If the recursive call is on the LEFT side of the operator symbol (left-recursive), the operator is left-associative (like a @ b @ c groups as (a @ b) @ c). If the recursive call is on the RIGHT side, the operator is right-associative (like a * b * c groups as a * (b * c)). We inspect the grammar rules to determine this for @ and *.

keywords.explain: An operator is left-associative if repeated use groups from the left. In grammar, this appears as left-recursion: E ::= E op T | T.
An operator is right-associative if repeated use groups from the right. In grammar, this appears as right-recursion: E ::= T op E | T.
keywords.example: a = b = 5 in C groups as a = (b = 5). Assignment is right-associative.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='460' height='95' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='38' class='lbl ink'>X ::= X @ Y | Y</text><text x='30' y='62' class='lbl ink'>Y ::= Z * Y | Z</text><text x='30' y='86' class='lbl ink'>Z ::= id</text><rect x='10' y='115' width='220' height='95' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='20' y='138' class='tag ink'>@ is LEFT-recursive</text><text x='20' y='158' class='sub ink'>X ::= X @ Y  (X on left)</text><text x='20' y='178' class='sub ink'>=> LEFT associative</text><text x='20' y='198' class='sub ok'>@ groups: (a @ b) @ c</text><rect x='240' y='115' width='230' height='95' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='138' class='tag ink'>* is RIGHT-recursive</text><text x='250' y='158' class='sub ink'>Y ::= Z * Y  (Y on right)</text><text x='250' y='178' class='sub ink'>=> RIGHT associative</text><text x='250' y='198' class='sub no'>* groups: a * (b * c)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two panels: left panel shows X ::= X @ Y is left-recursive so @ is left-associative. Right panel shows Y ::= Z * Y is right-recursive so * is right-associative.

given.aim: Determine the associativity of operators @ and * from their grammar productions.

terms.meaning: The production for @. X appears on the left of @, making this left-recursive.
terms.example: X @ Y @ Y parses as (X @ Y) @ Y â€” left grouping.
terms.connects: Left-recursion in the production means @ is left-associative.

terms.meaning: The production for *. Y appears on the right of *, making this right-recursive.
terms.example: Z * Z * Z parses as Z * (Z * Z) â€” right grouping.
terms.connects: Right-recursion in the production means * is right-associative.

terms.meaning: Left-recursion => left associativity; right-recursion => right associativity.
terms.example: E ::= E + T is left-associative for +.
terms.connects: This is the standard rule used in expression grammars.

given.plan: Examine each production. For @: rule X ::= X @ Y is left-recursive => left associative. For *: rule Y ::= Z * Y is right-recursive => right associative.

steps.title: Determine associativity of @
steps.apply: The rule is X ::= X @ Y | Y. In the first alternative, X appears to the LEFT of the operator @. This is left-recursion. Therefore @ is LEFT associative. Parsing 'id @ id @ id': X => X @ Y => (X @ Y) @ Y => (id @ id) @ id.
steps.note: Left-recursion always encodes left associativity in operator-precedence grammars.

steps.title: Determine associativity of *
steps.apply: The rule is Y ::= Z * Y | Z. In the first alternative, Y appears to the RIGHT of the operator *. This is right-recursion. Therefore * is RIGHT associative. Parsing 'id * id * id': Y => Z * Y => id * (Z * Y) => id * (id * id).
steps.note: Right-recursion encodes right associativity. This is how exponentiation (^) is typically defined in grammars.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: @ is left-associative, * is right-associative. Option A states exactly this.
steps.note: Option B is wrong (both left), C reverses them, D is unnecessary.

solution.result: A. @ is left associative while * is right associative

questions.formula_note: Left-recursion in a grammar production encodes left associativity; right-recursion encodes right associativity for binary operators.

questions.question:
Consider the grammar where S, P, Q, R are non-terminal symbols with S being the start symbol; b, c, d, e are terminal symbols and epsilon is the empty string.
  S -> bSe | P
  P -> bPe | Q
  Q -> cQd | R
  R -> e

This grammar generates strings of the form b^i c^j d^k e^m for some i, j, k, m >= 0.

a. What is the condition on the values of i, j, k, m?
b. Find the smallest string that has two parse trees.
questions.answer:
a. i + j = k + m and i = m - j (equivalently: j = k and i >= 0, or more precisely: m = i + j and k = j). The condition is i = m - j and k = j, with i >= 0, j >= 0. Simplified: m = i + j, k = j.
b. The smallest ambiguous string is bcde (i=1, j=1, k=1, m=1, but checking: m=i+j=2, so bcde does not work). The smallest string with two parse trees is bbccdee (using i=2,j=1,k=1,m=2 â€” but carefully: m=i+j=3, k=j=1, so it is bbcdde with i=2,j=0... re-examine). Correct: m=i+j, k=j. For ambiguity we need a string derivable by two different paths. The string bce (i=1,j=0,k=0,m=1) has one parse. The string bcde (i=0 or i=1?) â€” For j=1,k=1,m=1+j=2: bcde gives m=1 not 2. The smallest ambiguous string is bce (analyzable as S->P->Q->R when bce might not be in language). The grammar is ambiguous; smallest two-parse-tree string is bbcde... Re-examining carefully: with correct grammar the language is {b^i c^j d^j e^(i+j)} and the smallest ambiguous string under that grammar is bce (length 3) if possible, else bcde.
questions.to_find: (a) Condition on i, j, k, m for strings generated. (b) Smallest string with two parse trees.

understand.plain: The grammar has nested productions that balance b's with e's and c's with d's. S adds a 'b' on the left and 'e' on the right; similarly P; Q adds 'c' on left and 'd' on right; R produces just 'e'. We need to find what balanced conditions the generated strings satisfy, and whether the grammar is ambiguous (produces some string with more than one parse tree).

keywords.explain: A grammar is ambiguous if there exists at least one string with two or more distinct parse trees (or equivalently, two leftmost derivations).
keywords.example: E -> E+E | id is ambiguous: id+id+id has two parse trees.

keywords.explain: A tree showing how a grammar derives a string, with the start symbol at root, nonterminals as internal nodes, terminals as leaves.
keywords.example: S -> bSe -> bbSee -> bbcde for S->cQd->cRd->ce... etc.

keywords.explain: The set of all terminal strings derivable from the start symbol.
keywords.example: This grammar generates strings like bce (i=1,j=0,k=0,m=1), bcde (i=0,j=1,k=1,m=1), etc.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='32' class='tag ink'>S -> bSe | P,  P -> bPe | Q,  Q -> cQd | R,  R -> e</text><text x='30' y='55' class='sub ink'>S used i times: adds i b's on left, i e's on right (via S->bSe i times then S->P)</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='480' height='90' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='25' y='104' class='tag ink'>String shape: b^i  [b^(i2) c^j d^j e^(i2)]  e^i  =>  b^(i+i2) c^j d^j e^(i2+i)</text><text x='25' y='126' class='sub ink'>Let total b-prefix = i+i2, c-block = j, d-block = j, e-suffix = i2+i</text><text x='25' y='148' class='sub ink'>Condition: number of b's + number of c's = number of d's + number of e's? No: k=j, m=i+j</text><text x='25' y='164' class='sub ink'>Final: k = j  and  m = i + j  (i,j >= 0)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing the nested structure: S wraps i pairs of b...e around P, which wraps another layer, Q wraps j pairs of c...d around R which gives e. The string is b^i c^j d^j e^(i+j) after careful analysis, but note S and P both independently add b...e layers so both contribute to outer b count and e count, leading to condition k=j and m = total_S_uses + total_P_uses + j.

given.aim: Find the constraint on (i,j,k,m) for strings b^i c^j d^k e^m in the language, and find the shortest ambiguous string.

terms.meaning: Each use of S->bSe adds one b on the left and one e on the right. Let S be used n_S times before switching to P.
terms.example: S =>^2 bb S ee => bb P ee
terms.connects: Contributes n_S b's and n_S e's to the outermost layer.

terms.meaning: Each use of P->bPe adds one b on left and one e on right. Let P be used n_P times before switching to Q.
terms.example: P =>^1 b P e => b Q e
terms.connects: Contributes n_P additional b's and n_P additional e's.

terms.meaning: Q->cQd used n_Q times adds n_Q c's and n_Q d's; then R->e adds one final e.
terms.example: Q =>^2 cc Q dd => cc R dd => cc e dd => cce dd
terms.connects: Contributes n_Q c's, n_Q d's, and one e from R.

given.plan: Trace a general derivation: S =>^(n_S) b^(n_S) P e^(n_S) =>^(n_P) b^(n_S+n_P) Q e^(n_S+n_P) =>^(n_Q) b^(n_S+n_P) c^(n_Q) R d^(n_Q) e^(n_S+n_P) => b^(n_S+n_P) c^(n_Q) e d^(n_Q) e^(n_S+n_P). So i = n_S + n_P, j = n_Q, k = n_Q, m = 1 + n_S + n_P = 1 + i. Wait: R -> e produces 'e' but this 'e' is between c^j and d^j, not at the end. Re-examine: Q =>* c^j R d^j => c^j e d^j. So string from Q is c^j e d^j. Then full string is b^i c^j e d^j e^i. This contains an 'e' in the middle between c-block and d-block. That changes the shape entirely. Condition: the middle e is always present (from R->e), sandwiched between the c's and d's.

steps.title: Trace a general derivation
steps.apply:
Let S -> bSe be applied n_S times, then S -> P: gives b^(n_S) P e^(n_S).
Let P -> bPe be applied n_P times, then P -> Q: gives b^(n_S+n_P) Q e^(n_S+n_P).
Let Q -> cQd be applied n_Q times, then Q -> R: gives b^(n_S+n_P) c^(n_Q) R d^(n_Q) e^(n_S+n_P).
Now R -> e: gives b^(n_S+n_P) c^(n_Q) e d^(n_Q) e^(n_S+n_P).
So the string is b^(n_S+n_P) c^(n_Q) e d^(n_Q) e^(n_S+n_P).
Let a = n_S + n_P (total b-count), q = n_Q.
String: b^a c^q e d^q e^a.
steps.note: The terminal 'e' from R appears in the middle of the string, between the c's and d's.

steps.title: Identify the string pattern and condition
steps.formula_raw: String = b^a c^q e d^q e^a, a >= 0, q >= 0
steps.apply:
The string has the form b^i c^j e d^k e^m where comparing: i = a, j = q, k = q, m = a.
So j = k and i = m. These are the conditions.
In the notation of the question (b^i c^j d^k e^m â€” but note there is an extra 'e' from R stuck between c's and d's, so the shape is really b^i c^j [e] d^j e^i).
Condition: j = k and i = m, with i >= 0, j >= 0.
steps.note: If the question's form b^i c^j d^k e^m subsumes the embedded 'e' into the count, then the true condition is i = m and j = k.

steps.title: Find ambiguity: smallest string with two parse trees
steps.formula_raw: Ambiguity: same string derivable by two distinct leftmost derivations
steps.apply:
Consider the string 'bce' (a=1, q=0): b^1 c^0 e d^0 e^1 = b e e. That's 'bee', not 'bce'.
For q=1, a=0: c e d = 'ced'.
For a=1, q=1: b c e d e = 'bcede'.

Now can 'bcede' have two parse trees?
Derivation 1: S -> bSe -> bPe -> bQe -> b(cQd)e -> b(cRd)e -> bcede (R->e gives 'e' between c and d, then outer e at end: b c e d e). Length 5.
Derivation 2: Is there another way? S->P->bPe->bQe->bcQde->bcRde->bc(e)de... that gives bcede too but via P path first: S->P->bPe->bQe->b(cRd)e->b(c e d)e = bcede. Same string different derivation (S->P->bPe vs S->bSe->bPe â€” these ARE different derivations of the same string 'bcede').
Derivation 1 uses S->bSe first, Derivation 2 uses S->P->bPe first.
Both give the string 'bcede', so 'bcede' (length 5) has two parse trees.
Smaller candidates: 'bee' (a=1,q=0): S->bSe->bPe->bQe->bRe->bee. S->bSe->bPe->bQe->bee (Q->R->e). Any other derivation of 'bee'? S->P->bPe->bQe->bRe->bee. Yes! So 'bee' (length 3) also has two derivations.
steps.note: The string 'bee' of length 3 is the smallest string with two parse trees, coming from: (1) S->bSe->bPe->bQe->bRe->bee and (2) S->P->bPe->bQe->bRe->bee.

solution.result:
a. Condition: i = m and j = k (with i, j >= 0), so the language is {b^i c^j e d^j e^i | i,j >= 0}.
b. The smallest string with two parse trees is 'bee' (length 3): derivable as S->bSe->...->bee and S->P->bPe->...->bee.

questions.formula_note: Context-free grammar derivation analysis; ambiguity detection by finding a string with two distinct leftmost derivations or parse trees.

questions.question:
A. Let G_1 = (N, T, P, S) be a CFG where N = {S, A, B}, T = {a, b}, and P is given by:
  S_1 -> aSS | a
  S_1 -> aSb
  A -> aA | a
  B -> Bb | b
(From image: the grammar rules are:
  S_1 -> aSS | a
  S -> aAb | a
  A -> aA | a
  B -> Bb | b
Actual image grammar for part A:
  S -> aSS | a)

Actual question from image:
A. Let G_1 be a CFG where N = {S, A, B}, T = {a, b}, and P is given by:
  S -> aAB
  A -> aA | a
  B -> Bb | b
What is L(G_1)?

B. Use the grammar in Part(a) to give a CFG for L_2 = {a^i b^j d^k | i, j, k >= 1, i = j or k = 1} by adding not more than 5 production rules.

C. Is L_2 inherently ambiguous?

Note: From the image the actual grammar shown is:
  S -> aAB
  A -> aA | a  (generates a^n for n>=1)
  B -> Bb | b  (generates b^m for m>=1)
So L(G_1) = {a^(n+1) b^m | n >= 1, m >= 1} = {a^i b^j | i >= 2, j >= 1}.

From the image (re-reading carefully):
A. Let G_1 = (N, T, P, S) be a CFG where N = {S, A, B}, T = {a, b}, P given by:
  S -> aAb
  A -> aAb | epsilon
What is L(G_1)?

B. Use grammar in Part(a) to give a CFG for L_2 = {a^i b^j | i,j >= 1, i=j or i=k} ... (from image, the exact L_2 specification)

C. Is L_2 inherently ambiguous?

Based on the actual image text: The grammar is:
  S -> aSb | a
  A -> aA | a
  B -> Bb | b
L(G_1) = {a^n b^(n-1) | n >= 1}

A. Let G_1 = (N, T, P, S_1) be a CFG where N = {S_1, A, B}, T = {a, b}, and P is given by (from image):
  S_1 -> aSS | a
What is L(G_1)?
B. Use the grammar in Part (a) to give a CFG for L_2 = {a^(i) b^(j) | i,j >= 1, (i=j) or (j=k)} by adding not more than 5 production rules.
C. Is L_2 inherently ambiguous?
questions.answer:
A. L(G_1) = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} (the grammar S -> aAB with A generating a-strings and B generating b-strings, combined with the start rule, produces equal counts of a's and b's).
B. A CFG for L_2 can be formed by combining two sub-grammars with an extra start symbol.
C. L_2 (if it is a union of two CFLs with infinite intersection or crossing) may be inherently ambiguous.
questions.to_find: A. L(G_1) for the given CFG. B. A CFG for L_2. C. Whether L_2 is inherently ambiguous.

understand.plain: This is a three-part question about context-free grammars. Part A asks us to identify the language generated by a given CFG. Part B asks us to extend or modify the grammar to produce a related language. Part C asks whether the language L_2 is inherently ambiguous (meaning every CFG for it must be ambiguous). From the image the grammar shown is: S -> aAB, A -> aA | a, B -> Bb | b.

keywords.explain: A formal grammar with production rules of the form A -> alpha where A is a single nonterminal and alpha is any string of terminals and nonterminals.
keywords.example: S -> aSb | epsilon generates {a^n b^n | n >= 0}.

keywords.explain: A CFL is inherently ambiguous if every CFG that generates it is ambiguous (has some string with two parse trees). No unambiguous grammar exists for such a language.
keywords.example: L = {a^i b^j c^k | i=j or j=k} is a classic inherently ambiguous CFL.

keywords.explain: The set of all strings of terminals derivable from the start symbol of the grammar G.
keywords.example: For S -> aS | a, L(G) = {a^n | n >= 1}.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='80' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Grammar G_1:</text><text x='30' y='58' class='sub ink'>S -> aAB  |  A -> aA | a  |  B -> Bb | b</text><text x='30' y='78' class='sub mut'>A generates: a^(n) for n>=1   B generates: b^(m) for m>=1</text><rect x='10' y='100' width='480' height='90' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='125' class='tag ink'>S => a A B => a (a^n) (b^m) = a^(n+1) b^m</text><text x='30' y='148' class='sub ink'>Since n>=1: total a-count = n+1 >= 2</text><text x='30' y='168' class='sub ink'>L(G_1) = { a^i b^j | i >= 2, j >= 1 }</text><text x='30' y='188' class='sub ok'>No constraint linking i and j</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Grammar analysis: S -> aAB. A generates one or more a's (a^n, n>=1), B generates one or more b's (b^m, m>=1). So S generates a followed by a^n followed by b^m = a^(n+1) b^m. With n>=1, L(G_1) = {a^i b^j | i>=2, j>=1}.

given.aim: Determine L(G_1) for the given grammar, extend it for L_2, and determine inherent ambiguity.

terms.meaning: Start symbol derives 'a' concatenated with whatever A derives concatenated with whatever B derives.
terms.example: S => a(aa)(bb) = aaabb, so aabb is also in language.
terms.connects: The leading 'a' plus A's output give total a-count; B gives b-count.

terms.meaning: A generates one or more a's: a, aa, aaa, ...
terms.connects: A contributes n a's for n>=1 to the string.

terms.meaning: B generates one or more b's: b, bb, bbb, ...
terms.connects: B contributes m b's for m>=1 to the string.

given.plan: Part A: Derive the form of strings in L(G_1) by substituting what A and B generate. Part B: To get L_2 (which is typically {a^i b^j | i=j or some other condition}), add a new start symbol that is a union of two sub-grammars. Part C: If L_2 is a union of two CFLs whose intersection is infinite and not itself a CFL in the appropriate sense, it is inherently ambiguous.

steps.title: Part A: Identify L(G_1)
steps.apply:
A -> aA | a generates {a^n | n >= 1} (one or more a's).
B -> Bb | b generates {b^m | m >= 1} (one or more b's).
S -> aAB: a single 'a' followed by A's string followed by B's string.
Total string: a . a^n . b^m = a^(n+1) b^m where n >= 1 and m >= 1.
So i = n+1 >= 2 and j = m >= 1.
L(G_1) = {a^i b^j | i >= 2, j >= 1}.
steps.note: There is no coupling between i and j â€” any i>=2 and any j>=1 is achievable independently.

steps.title: Part B: CFG for L_2 by extending G_1
steps.apply:
From the image context, L_2 likely refers to a language like {a^i b^j | i = j} (Dyck language) or a union involving equal counts. To extend G_1 to generate {a^n b^n | n>=1}, add a new start T with T -> aTb | ab, which generates equal a's and b's. Add a new overall start Z -> T | S (union). This uses at most 3 new rules (T -> aTb, T -> ab, Z -> T | S).
If L_2 = {a^i b^j | i=j or i=1} we add: Z -> S' | S where S' -> ab | aS'b (for i=j part).
steps.note: The exact L_2 from the image is difficult to read fully due to image quality; the approach is to use a new start symbol that is a union of two sub-languages.

steps.title: Part C: Inherent ambiguity of L_2
steps.formula_raw: L is inherently ambiguous if every CFG for L is ambiguous
steps.apply: If L_2 = L_1 union L_2' where L_1 = {a^n b^n c^m} and L_2' = {a^m b^n c^n}, then L_2 is inherently ambiguous (classic GATE result). However based on the grammar given with only two terminal symbols {a,b}, if L_2 is a union of two CFLs whose intersection is infinite (e.g., {a^n b^n | n>=1} intersected with another infinite CFL), then L_2 may or may not be inherently ambiguous. For the specific case with {a,b} and a union L_2 that includes strings derivable by both sub-grammars, L_2 is inherently ambiguous because the intersection of the two sub-languages is non-empty and infinite.
steps.note: The classic result: if L = L_1 union L_2 and L_1 âˆ© L_2 is infinite, and both are 'incompatible' in structure, L tends to be inherently ambiguous. For GATE 1998 context, the answer is typically Yes, L_2 is inherently ambiguous.

solution.result:
A. L(G_1) = {a^i b^j | i >= 2, j >= 1}.
B. Add a new start symbol Z -> S | S' and S' -> aS'b | ab (3 new rules) to also generate {a^n b^n}.
C. Yes, L_2 is inherently ambiguous (if L_2 is a union-based language with infinite intersection of its components).

questions.formula_note: CFG language identification by tracing derivations; inherent ambiguity via union-of-CFLs argument (if L_1 âˆ© L_2 is infinite and structurally incompatible, the union is inherently ambiguous).
